coredata.dc:title: Polysulfonamide coating layer polymerized by1,3-disulfonyl chloride and polyethylenimine to achieve acid resistant TFC membranes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Institution of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Institution of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Bali Eslami, Ali
Peyravi, Majid
Jahanshahi, Mohsen
Hosseinpour, Hamzeh
coredata.dc:description: In this work polysulfonamide membranes are presented that are prepared by the interfacial polymerization of polyethylenimine (PEI) and benzene-1,3-disulfonyl chloride (BDSC) on porous polyvinylidenefluorid support membrane. The thin ﬁlm composite (TFC) polysulfonamide membranes show superior acid stability as compared to conventional polysulfonamide membranes that are derived from monomeric amine and benzene-1,3-disulfonyl chloride. The polysulfonamide membranes show exceptionally strong resistance towards nucleophilic attack induced by extreme acidic conditions. This is veriﬁed by the dyes rejection performance in 20%(w/v) H2SO4 and 2.5%(w/v) HNO3 aqueous solution at 70 ± 5 °C for 24 h. PEI-PSA and PEI/PIP-PSA membranes were considerably stable in two acids. In contrast, the durability of PEI/MPD-PSA was lower than the other two. The changes in CV and MO dye rejection were −3.15% and −2.19%, and −1.11%, −5.6% for PEI-PSA and PEI/PIP-PSA, respectively after H2SO4 soaking while they were −20% and −15.9% for PEI/MPD-PSA. Moreover, the changes in CV and MO dye rejection were −15.7% and −14.3%, and −7.8%, −5.7 % for PEI-PSA and PEI/PIP-PSA, respectively after HNO3 soaking while they were −17.7% and −10.2% for PEI/MPD-PSA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thin film composite membrane
Polysulfonamide
Interfacial polymerization
Acid stability
Durability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the acid-stable TFC membranes

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2020 Institution of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Surface AFM images of (a) PVDF substrate (b) PSA membranes PEI (c) PSA membranes PEI/MPD (d) PSA membranes PEI/PIP.
SEM image of the surface of (a) PVDF, (b) PEI-PSA, (c) PEI/MPD-PSA, d) PEI/PIP-PSA and cross sectional of (e) PVDF, (f) PEI-PSA, (g) PEI/MPD-PSA, (h) PEI/PIP-PSA.
Surface contact angle of the PVDF support and PSA membranes.
Pure water flux of PSA membranes under 5 bar.
Dye rejections of PSA membranes before acid soaking tests under 5 bar for 10 ppm CV and MO dye aqueous solution.
The structural image of PIP-BDSC and MPD-BDSC polymer and the location of nucleophile attack to sulfur atom.
Pure water flux (a) and dye rejection (b) of PSA membranes under 5 bar after acid soaking in 20% (w/w) H2SO4 at 70 ± 5 °C for 24 h. The dyes concentrations in aqueous solution were 10 ppm.
Pure water flux (a) and dye rejection (b) of PSA membranes under 5 bar after acid soaking in 2.5% (w/w) HNO3 at 70 ± 5 °C for 24 h. The dyes concentrations in aqueous solution were 10 ppm.
The composition of the aqueous and organic solutions for preparation of PSA membrane.
Root mean square roughness of PVDF substrate and PSA composite membranes.

head.ce:title: Polysulfonamide coating layer polymerized by1,3-disulfonyl chloride and polyethylenimine to achieve acid resistant TFC membranes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Babol Noshirvani University of Technology, Shariati Ave., Babol, 47148-71167, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nuclear Science and Technology Research Institute (NSTRI), Tehran, Iran.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemical Engineering, Babol Noshirvani University of Technology, Shariati Av., P.O. Box: 484, Babol, 47148-71167, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work polysulfonamide membranes are presented that are prepared by the interfacial polymerization of polyethylenimine (PEI) and benzene-1,3-disulfonyl chloride (BDSC) on porous polyvinylidenefluorid support membrane. The thin ﬁlm composite (TFC) polysulfonamide membranes show superior acid stability as compared to conventional polysulfonamide membranes that are derived from monomeric amine and benzene-1,3-disulfonyl chloride. The polysulfonamide membranes show exceptionally strong resistance towards nucleophilic attack induced by extreme acidic conditions. This is veriﬁed by the dyes rejection performance in 20%(w/v) H2SO4 and 2.5%(w/v) HNO3 aqueous solution at 70 ± 5 °C for 24 h. PEI-PSA and PEI/PIP-PSA membranes were considerably stable in two acids. In contrast, the durability of PEI/MPD-PSA was lower than the other two. The changes in CV and MO dye rejection were −3.15% and −2.19%, and −1.11%, −5.6% for PEI-PSA and PEI/PIP-PSA, respectively after H2SO4 soaking while they were −20% and −15.9% for PEI/MPD-PSA. Moreover, the changes in CV and MO dye rejection were −15.7% and −14.3%, and −7.8%, −5.7 % for PEI-PSA and PEI/PIP-PSA, respectively after HNO3 soaking while they were −17.7% and −10.2% for PEI/MPD-PSA.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanofiltration is of the most widely known techniques for water and wastewater treatment, bio-separation and seawater desalination. Nanofiltration membranes are used for separation of small molecules with MW cut-off approximately from 200 to 1000 Da (Ali and Suhaimi, 2009; Hoseinpour et al., 2017; Luo and Wan, 2013; Mohammad et al., 2015).

Nanofiltration membrane performance in separation relies largely on the membrane properties. Several modifications were done on the thin layer formation process. These include layer by layer (LBL) development, thin film coating and random polymerization. LBL method increases flux however it doesn’t significant rejection.one on the other method is confining migration of amine monomer during interfacial polymerization with low fouling propensity. this method despite increases flux, reduces fouling tendency (Dong and Ge, 2019; Jiang et al., 2019; long Xue et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019). Thin-film composite (TFC) NF membranes have been confirmed to be among the successful and more efficient categories of nanofiltration membranes (Chung et al., 2019; Gai et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2012; Zhao and Chung, 2018). However, a challenge for commonly used nanoﬁltration (NF) membranes is their weakness in strong acidic medias. Industries, which are involved with harsh acidic solutions such as pulp and paper industry, textile dyeing process and extraction process, can reap significant beneﬁts through the treatment of these wastewaters. Facing this challenge needs acid stable membranes to keep their separation ability in a wide range of acidic pH without any considerable change in MWCOs (Lee et al., 2017, 2015; Liu et al., 2012).

Unfortunately, the commercial NF membranes derived from polyamide (PA) and cellulose acetate (CA) lose their favorable performance due to the low durability amide bonds against strong acids in long-term applications. On the contrary, polysulfonamides (PSAs) are a kind of polymer with high thermal and chemical stability due to the presence of sulfonyl and phenyl groups and delocalization (Soulier et al., 1974). The major difference between PSAs and PAs which made PSAs highly renitent to nucleophilic acid attacks is the substitution of sensitive CO bonds in PAs with SO bonds in PSAs (Liu et al., 2012).

There are few researches studying fabrication and evaluating the acid stability of different polysulfonamide TFC membranes via interfacial polymerization (IP) method. Kurth et al. (2006) prepared the acid resistant ploysulfonamide TFC nanofiltration membrane via in situ IP of triethylenetetramine in water with naphthalene-1,3,6-trisulfonylchloride dissolved in Isopar G. The obtained ploysulfonamide membranes exhibited excellent acid durability in 20% (w/v) H2SO4 aqueous solution (Suter, 1944).

Yu et al. (2013) prepared PSA TFC membrane using piperazine (PIP) in aqueous solution and naphthalene-1,3,6-trisulfonylchloride (NTSC) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) as cross-linking agents in organic phase. The membrane derived from TMC revealed major performance change in comparison with the synthesized membrane after filtration with 4.9% w/v H2SO4 for 60 days or immersion in 8% w/v H2SO4 for 30 days. In our previous work (Hoseinpour et al., 2016), poly(amide-sulfonamide) and PSA acid-stable membranes were fabricated by IP of different diamines with benzene-1,3-disulfonyl chloride (BDSC) and/or TMC on polyethersulfone (PES) support membrane. The PSA membranes were found to exhibit high acid stability in 10%(w/v) H2SO4 and 2.5%(w/v) HNO3 aqueous solution at 55 °C for 24 h.

However, in the mentioned researches the selected amines were of monomeric ones while it was proved that polymeric amines show excellent durability in harsh acidic conditions when they react with appropriate cross-linkers (Hoseinpour et al., 2017). Polyethylenimine (PEI) is one of the most suitable polymeric amines which forms thicker top layer compared to membranes based on small monomeric amines (Cadotte et al., 1981).

The preparation of novel PSA TFC nanoﬁltration membrane by improved acid stability was proceeded via in situ IP of BDSC and PEI on porous polyvinylidenefluoride (PVDF) support membrane. Moreover, to increase the cross-linking degree in the structure of top selective layer, monomeric PIP and m-phenylenediamine (MPD) amines were used together with PEI as auxiliary agents. The separation performance and acid stability of the resulted PSA membrane were evaluated via cross-flow filtration experiments. To improve the rate of the degradation process, which under real conditions would take a long time, membranes were exposed to 20% sulfuric acid and 2.5% nitric acid at the high temperature of 70 °C. Membranes morphology was determined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). The chemical structure of the top layer was characterized by attenuated reflectance infrared (ATR-IR).

To fabricate the support membrane, PVDF was provided by Merck. Dimethylformamide (DMF) as the PVDF solvent and poly (vinyl pyrrolidone) (PVP), as a pore former in asymmetric membrane were supplied by Merck. Organic solvent, Isopar G (Isoparaffin type hydrocarbon oil), and diamine monomers such as PIP and MPD were purchased from Merck. Branched PEI (Mn = 10,000, Mw = 25,000) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. BDSC (97%) as cross-linking agent was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Crystsal violet (CV) as cationic dye and methyl orange (MO) as anionic dye were supplied by Merck. Pure water was used in this study.

In order to prepare the TFC membrane, at first, an ultrafiltration support membrane was fabricated using immersion precipitation method (Mertens et al., 2018). The casting solution composed of 16% PVDF and 1% PVP in 83% DMF. PVP also plays the role of pore former during the membrane synthesis by phase inversion. Depending on the polymer properties and the additive-to-polymer ratio it is therefore possible to prepare membranes with different morphologies (pore structure) and hydrophilicities, and then with different performance in terms of permeability and rejection. A magnetic stirrer homogenized the mixture for 24 h at 27 °C. Then, the mixture was cooled and degassed at room temperature to eliminate the bubbles. The dope solution was cast on non-woven commercial polyester by a casting knife with a 150 μm knife gap. It was immersed in gelation bath comprising water as the noN–Solvent at 25 °C for 24 h to complete phase inversion.

Three different kinds of PSA NF membranes were synthesized through IP between amine solution and BDSC on the surface of the PVDF membrane (Ramezani Darabi et al., 2018). The amine solution contained PEI or the mixture of PEI and PIP or MPD. The support membrane was immersed for 30 min in amine solution. Then in order to eliminate excess amine solution, it was drawn out. The excess aqueous solution was eliminated from the support using a rubbery wiper. Then the saturated membrane was immersed in 25.0 °C organic solution comprising BDSC in Isopar G for the 30 min. To complete the formation process of the thin film, the membranes were cured at 90 °C for about 10 min. The composition of the prepared membranes was listed in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the acid-stable TFC membranes

ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the surface roughness and topography of the prepared membranes, Atomic force microscopy (Nanosurf AG, Switzerland) device with a scan area 5 μm × 5 μm was applied. The membrane small pieces (about 1 cm2) fixed on glass plate and were scanned. The surface roughness was represented in terms of root mean square (RMS).

The water contact angle of the surface of pristine PVDF and TFC membranes were measured by contact angle analyzer (KRUSS BmbH, Germany). In all measurements, about 3 μl de-ionized water was poured on the surface of dried membrane at room temperature. To reduce the experimental error, the average contact angle of four different locations was reported.

The acid stability of membranes was evaluated by soaking the membranes in acute acid solutions for 24 h (Platt et al., 2004). The soaking tests were accomplished in a glass flask equipped with a reflux system to avoid initial acid concentration change by vaporization at high temperatures. The TFC membranes were immersed individually in 2.5% (w/w) HNO3 and 20.0% (w/w) H2SO4 aqueous solutions at 70 ± 5 °C. After the test, the membranes samples were taken out of the acidic solutions, then washed using distilled water and held at ambient temperature to be used for filtration.

For investigating the performance of TFC membranes, a cross-flow filtration system with an effective filtration area of 40 cm2 at a pressure of 5 bar and temperature of 25 °C was used. The filtration performance of the membranes was evaluated by measuring the dye rejection (R) and pure water fluxes (F). Two kinds of dyes solutions were separately prepared. One with 10 ppm CV and another with 10 ppm MO in de-ionized water. Also, in all tests, de-ionized water was used.

The flux, F, was achieved as follows:
(1)
F =
V
A
Δ t
where V(L) is the volume of permeate, A(m2) is the membrane effective area and Δ t ( h ) is the operation time. The membranes rejection, R, was calculated using the bellow equation:
(2)
R ( % ) = ( 1 -
C P
C F
) × 100
where CP and CF are the concentrations of dye in permeate and feed solutions, respectively.
The solute dye concentration was measured using UV–Vis spectrophotometer (U-2800, Hitachi, Japan).

The surfaces topography of TFC composite membranes was determined by atomic force microscopy. The three-dimensional images of 5 μm × 5 μm scan of the membranes surfaces are illustrated in Fig. 1 . The surface roughness of the PVDF substrate and polysulfonamide membranes in terms of the root mean square roughness is reported in Table 2 . The surface of the PVDF substrate is rather smooth (Fig. 1a) with a roughness of only 20 nm. There are more ridges and valleys on the surface of the PSA membrane. It has been found that PSA top layer has least effects on both the cross section and surface morphology of pristine membrane (Yu et al., 2013). Therefore, the PSA membranes exhibited similar nodular structure to PVDF substrate with small-scale surface roughness. On the other hand, it has been proven that PEI makes smoother polymer when it is cross-linked in comparison with monomeric amines such as PIP and MPD (Wu et al., 2015). That is because PEI is a linear polymer with short molecular chain distances in which the amine groups are equally distributed. In contrast, PIP and MPD have cyclic structures that make rough surface when they are cross-linked by another aromatic molecule (BDSC). PEI/PIP-PSA surface is rougher than two other PSA membranes, which is because of the long distance between ridges and valleys on the surface of the membrane (Fig. 1b) (Hoseinpour et al., 2016).
The morphology of the pristine PVDF and PSA membranes are demonstrated in Fig. 2 . A fingerlike morphology can be observed from the cross-sectional image of PVDF support membrane.

As PSA selective layer too thin, the boundary between top layer and support membrane cannot be clearly distinguished from cross-sectional images (Liu et al., 2012). However, comparing the surface image of PVDF pristine membrane Fig. 2a and PSA membranes Fig. 2b–d) distinctly shows the change in surface morphology due to the formation of PSA top layer. As was confirmed by AFM analyses, adding monomeric amines in casting solution that contained only PEI, altered the structure and surface morphology PEI/MPD-PSA and membranes and made them rougher. However, PEI/PIP-PSA membrane had a completely different surface. This is because PIP with cycloaliphatic structure produces higher molecular weight PSA polymeric chains than aromatic MPD and so the roughness and shape of the PEI/PIP-PSA surface which are produced from the formation of a thin film, would increase (Hoseinpour et al., 2016).

The surface wettability of the thin film PSA membranes were investigated by measurement of the contact angle between the membrane surface and the air–water interface. The results are reported in Fig. 3 . As known, the membrane surface hydrophilicity depends on the chemical properties and roughness characteristics. The amine end groups, sulfonamide bonds and pendant sulfonic acid groups (from the hydrolysis of unreacted sulfonyl chloride groups) on the thin film layer are the main groups that increase its hydrophilicity. As shown in Fig. 3, the water contact angle of the PVDF support surface as a hydrophobic polymer was 85° and it decreased to 67° by PEI-PSA thin layer. However, when the PIP and MPD was added in amine solution, the contact angle decreased to 52° and 61, respectively. According to AFM results (Table 2), the PEI/PIP-PSA membrane (Sq = 60 nm) has much rougher surface than PEI/MPD-PSA one (Sq = 24.37 nm). As a result, the PEI/PIP-PSA membrane has more hydrophilic surface than PEI/MPD-PSA one.

Fig. 4 illustrates the pure water (deionized water) flux of TFC membranes at 5 bar transmembrane pressure. The manner of flux change for PSA membranes is as PEI-PSA < PEI/MPD-PSA < PEI/PIP-PSA. The pure water flux for PEI-PSA membrane was 16 kg/m2 h while it was 20 and 22 kg/m2 h for PEI/MPD-PSA and PEI/PIP-PSA, respectively. It has been found that the IP reaction occurs in organic phase and after the formation of primary thin layer, the diffusion of amines through it will control the growth and densifying the final thin layer (Jahanshahi et al., 2010). PEI is a branched macromolecule with a low partition coefficient in the organic phase. Therefore, it can hardly diffuse from the primary layer to the organic solution (Wu et al., 2015). Conversely, the small molecules of PIP and MPD diffuse more easily due to the higher mobility and reactivity and so react rapidly with BDSC and form PIP-BDSC and MPD-BDSC linkages. These linkages lead to the reduced diffusion and more cross-linking of PEI macromolecules with BDSC molecules. Consequently, the polymerisation rate between PEI and BDSC would become even slower and the resulted selective layer would be looser. This phenomenon along with hydrophilicity factor caused PEI/PIP-PSA and PEI/MPD-PSA to show higher flux than PEI-PSA. On the other hand the rougher surface and more hydrophilic surface endowed higher flux to PEI/PIP-PSA in contrast with PEI/MPD-PSA (McNaught and Wilkinson, 1997).

The dye rejection ability of the PSA membranes before static tests are depicted in Fig. 5 . The MO and CV dyes concentrations in aqueous solution were 10 ppm. The MO dye rejections of three membranes are nearly the same and about 90%. However, the CV dye rejection of PEI-PSA, PEI/PIP-PSA and PEI/MPD-PSA is 95%, 91% and 90%, respectively. Thus, the rejection rate of MO dye with lower molecular weight (327.334 g/mole) is nearly the same as CV dye with higher molecular weight (586.4 g/mole). It can be due to the Donnan exclusion effects (Miyoshi, 1997). The charged solutes can be separated in NF process pursuant to their size (steric size effect), charge density (Donnan exclusion effect) and mobility (Szymczyk and Fievet, 2005; Van der Bruggen et al., 1999). The surface charge of PSA composite membranes is negative at pH of 6.8 due to the dissociation of sulfonic acid groups from the hydrolysis of sulfonyl chloride groups (Hoseinpour et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2012; Yu et al., 2013). So, the Donnan exclusion effects between the negatively charged membrane surface and anionic dye molecules would increase the rejection of MO dye molecules despite of its low molecular weight.

The acid stability of the PSA membranes was evaluated through static acid soaking tests. The fabricated membranes were soaked separately in 20 w/v% H2SO4 and 2.5 w/v% HNO3 aqueous solution at 70 ± 5 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, they were washed completely with deionized water and their pure water flux and CV and MO dye rejection were evaluated. As the acid degradation at the room temperature need to a rather long time, the static tests done at high temperature to accelerate the hydrolysis process of acid (Freger et al., 2005). As an appropriate approximation, degradation reaction rate will double following every 10-degree enhancement in temperature (Kurth et al., 2006). Figs. 7 and 8 exhibits the pure water flux and dye rejection of PSA membranes after acid soaking tests. The results show that using PEI as a macromolecule amine polymer in IP reaction with BDSC results in an excellent acid stable polymer compared to monomeric amines such as PIP and MPD. For example, in our previous work, the reductions in salt rejection rate of PIP and MPD based PSA membranes were −9.6% and −22%, respectively after soaking in 5 w/v% H2SO4 at 55 °C (Hoseinpour et al., 2016). In contrast, in this research the reductions in the CV and MO dye rejection rate of PEI-PSA were −3.2% and −1.1%, respectively after soaking in 20 w/v% H2SO4 at 70 °C for PEI-PSA.

On the other hand, BDSC with stable sulfonyl groups is much more effective in acid stability than TMC with carbonyl groups when it cross-links the PEI polymeric chains. For instance, the reduction in salt rejection rate of PEI based PA membranes were −46% after soaking in 2.5 w/v% HNO3 at 55 °C, respectively (Hoseinpour et al., 2017). In contrast, in this research the reduction of the CV and MO dye rejection rate of PEI-PSA were −15.7% and −7.8%, respectively after soaking in 2.5 w/v% HNO3 at 70 °C.

PEI-PSA and PEI/PIP-PSA membranes were considerably stable in two acids. In contrast, the durability of PEI/MPD-PSA was lower than the other two. The changes in CV and MO dye rejection were −3.15% and −2.19%, and −1.11%, −5.6% for PEI-PSA and PEI/PIP-PSA, respectively after H2SO4 soaking while they were −20% and −15.9% for PEI/MPD-PSA. Moreover, the changes in CV and MO dye rejection were −15.7% and −14.3%, and −.8%, −5.7 % for PEI-PSA and PEI/PIP-PSA, respectively after HNO3 soaking while they were −17.7% and −10.2% for PEI/MPD-PSA.

The results show that adding PIP to amine solution, increases the acid stability of PEI based PSA membrane compared to MPD. Also, this demonstrates that the sulfonamide group obtained from PIP and BDSC is stronger than that obtained from MPD and BDSC. The reasons are as follows:

Three different kinds of PSA nanofiltration membranes have been successfully prepared by interfacial polymerisation of amine solution and benzene-1,3-disulfonyl chloride upon polyvinylidenefluorid support membrane. The amine solution contained PEI or the mixture of PEI and PIP or MPD. The acid-stable structures and performance were compared with our previous work. The results showed that the structure of the membranes is not similar to each other. Also, the results confirmed that performance of PEI-PSA and PEI/PIP-PSA membranes were quite different from the PEI/MPD-PSA membranes. Static acid soaking tests with 20% (w/w) H2SO4 and 2.5% (w/w) HNO3 at high temperature of 70 ± 5 °C indicated that using PEI as a macromolecule amine polymer in IP reaction with BDSC results in an excellent acid stable polymer compared to monomeric amines such as PIP and MPD.
I have no pecuniary or other personal interest, direct or indirect, in any matter that raises or may raise a conflict with the current manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the founding support of Babol Noshirvani University of Technology through Grant program No. BNUT/389026/98.
